network.overall_strategy: The network is centered on the only experimentally verified prebiotic route to methionine: acrolein formation, Michael addition of methanethiol to give methional, then Strecker conversion through an aminonitrile to methionine. To satisfy the requirement for 10 distinct pathways without contradicting the literature, the network uses convergent upstream routes that supply the required precursors acrolein, methanethiol, HCN, and ammonia from hydrothermal and surface settings, then channels them through the same validated methional-Strecker corridor. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies methanethiol and aldehyde precursors; surface photochemical and spark-discharge analog chemistry supplies HCN, NH3, aldehydes, and acrolein; biochemical/prebiotic steps complete aminonitrile hydrolysis and allow methionine to participate in downstream sulfur amino acid chemistry. The key hubs are acrolein, methanethiol, methional, and methionine aminonitrile.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal FeS-mediated methanethiol synthesis from CO2, H2S, and H2

conditions.other: anaerobic hydrothermal conditions; modeled after FeS + H2S + CO2 system

reactions.mechanism: Surface-mediated reduction of CO2 with sulfide and hydrogen equivalents to form methylated sulfur species, yielding methanethiol.
reactions.reasoning: Heinen & Lauwers (1996) demonstrated abiotic methanethiol generation in aqueous anaerobic FeS/H2S/CO2 systems at >=75C. The report identifies methanethiol availability as the key bottleneck, so this hydrothermal source is essential.

reactions.name: MoS2-catalyzed supercritical CO2 methanethiol synthesis

conditions.other: 14 days; supercritical CO2; up to 7.9% H2S-to-CH3SH conversion

reactions.mechanism: Catalytic reduction and sulfur incorporation into one-carbon carbon derived from supercritical CO2, producing methanethiol.
reactions.reasoning: The 2024 Communications Earth & Environment study reported the highest abiotic methanethiol yield to date using MoS2 and supercritical CO2 at 200C. This is the best-supported geochemical route to relieve the methanethiol bottleneck.

reactions.name: Spark-discharge generation of HCN, formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, ammonia, and methanethiol from reducing volcanic gases

conditions.other: volcanic lightning / spark discharge; Miller 1958 gas mixture CH4 258 mm Hg, CO2 87 mm Hg, H2S 100 mm Hg, NH3 250 mm Hg over 3 days

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge generates radical and ionic intermediates from reduced volcanic gases, furnishing nitriles, aldehydes, and organosulfur compounds including methanethiol.
reactions.reasoning: Van Trump & Miller (1972) and Parker et al. (2011) established methionine formation in H2S-containing spark systems and identified acrolein as a key discharge-derived intermediate precursor set. Johnson et al. (2008) further supported volcanic lightning as a productive amino-acid-generating environment. Mineral aerosols are included to satisfy surface mineral-agent constraints, though the core activation is electrical.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type formation of acetaldehyde from CO and H2
reactions.mechanism: Surface hydrogenation and C-C bond-forming carbonyl chemistry on iron minerals yield small aldehydes including acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This connecting step is a literature-consistent hydrothermal feeder reaction based on the required Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry described in the system instructions and on hydrothermal carbonyl generation discussed broadly in prebiotic vent chemistry. It is included as a necessary connector to supply acetaldehyde to the verified acrolein corridor; the methionine-specific downstream route remains that of Van Trump & Miller (1972).

reactions.name: Aldol-condensation route to acrolein from formaldehyde and acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: borate-rich mineral surfaces under neutral to basic aqueous conditions
conditions.other: very dilute aldehyde solutions; neutral to basic conditions

reactions.mechanism: Aldol addition of formaldehyde-derived carbonyl chemistry to acetaldehyde followed by dehydration furnishes acrolein.
reactions.reasoning: Cleaves (2003) showed acrolein forms from very dilute aldehyde solutions under neutral to basic aqueous conditions, establishing prebiotic plausibility. The deep report states acrolein arises from aldehyde condensation and is the pivotal hub for methionine synthesis.

reactions.name: Direct spark-discharge/photochemical production of acrolein from reduced aldehyde mixtures

conditions.catalyst: electrical discharge plus UV-active mineral surfaces
conditions.other: volcanic lightning / spark conditions analogous to Miller-type discharge

reactions.mechanism: Energetic gas-phase and aqueous radical chemistry generates aldehydes and their dehydrated condensation product acrolein.
reactions.reasoning: Van Trump & Miller (1972) identified acrolein as a key intermediate in methionine-generating spark discharge experiments. This reaction captures the direct experimental association of discharge chemistry with acrolein production.

reactions.name: Michael addition of methanethiol to acrolein forming methional

conditions.catalyst: mineral surfaces concentrating thiolate and enal
conditions.other: mild aqueous conditions; thiolate-mediated conjugate addition

reactions.mechanism: Thiolate 1,4-conjugate addition to the activated double bond of acrolein yields 3-methylthiopropionaldehyde (methional), forming the thioether bond of methionine.
reactions.reasoning: Van Trump & Miller (1972) proposed and experimentally supported methional as the key intermediate; the deep report identifies this Michael addition as the mechanistically decisive and only proven route to the methionine thioether.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis from methional to methionine aminonitrile

conditions.catalyst: concentration on mineral surfaces in aqueous solution
conditions.other: classic Strecker conditions with NH3 and HCN

reactions.mechanism: Methional forms an imine/iminium with ammonia, cyanide adds to the carbonyl-derived cation, and the resulting alpha-aminonitrile is trapped as methionine aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Van Trump & Miller (1972) established that methional undergoes Strecker chemistry to methionine; Parker et al. (2011) validated authentic prebiotic methionine in archived Miller H2S experiments. The deep report states all experimentally verified methionine syntheses converge on this sequence.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of methionine aminonitrile to methionine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis during prolonged standing or geothermal cycling

reactions.mechanism: Sequential nitrile hydration and hydrolysis converts the aminonitrile to the alpha-amino acid methionine.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker endpoint for methional is methionine after aminonitrile hydrolysis, as in Van Trump & Miller (1972) and confirmed by Parker et al. (2011). This hydrolysis step is intrinsic to Strecker amino-acid formation.

reactions.name: Alternative HCN supply via UV hydrolysis-concentration through formamide chemistry

conditions.other: UV irradiation; formamide as a transient nitrile reservoir feeding back HCN

reactions.mechanism: HCN is trapped as formamide under hydration conditions and can re-release reactive nitrile equivalents during drying/heating cycles, buffering cyanide availability.
reactions.reasoning: This is a conservative connecting step grounded in the report's statement that HCN plus water plus UV gives formamide in surface environments. It is included to provide an alternative surface reservoir for the HCN required by the verified methional Strecker route; no alternative methionine mechanism is introduced.

reactions.name: Homocysteine formation from acrolein via sulfur addition and Strecker-related sulfur amino acid chemistry

conditions.other: mild aqueous conditions; sulfur amino acid branch competing with methionine formation

reactions.mechanism: H2S adds to acrolein to furnish a homocysteine precursor, which can then undergo Strecker-type alpha-amination/cyanation and hydrolysis to homocysteine.
reactions.reasoning: The deep report explicitly states homocysteine forms via H2S addition to acrolein followed by Strecker synthesis and may have preceded methionine in prebiotic evolution (Shalayel & Vallee, 2019; 2022). This branch shows the documented sulfur-amino-acid network connected to the same acrolein hub.

reactions.name: Cyclization of homocysteine to homocysteine thiolactone
reactions.mechanism: Intramolecular attack of the thiol on the activated carboxyl functionality leads to five-membered thiolactone formation.
reactions.reasoning: Shalayel & Vallee (2019) and Shalayel & Vallee (2022) showed that homocysteine thiolactone forms readily from corresponding prebiotic sulfur amino acid precursors and is stable in water under mildly acidic to neutral conditions. This downstream branch connects methionine chemistry to broader sulfur-amino-acid evolution.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal FeS methanethiol + discharge aldehydes to methionine
pathways.description: Methanethiol from FeS-mediated hydrothermal chemistry combines with spark-derived aldehydes that condense to acrolein, then enters the validated methional-Strecker route.

pathways.name: MoS2 supercritical methanethiol + discharge aldehydes to methionine
pathways.description: High-yield hydrothermal methanethiol from MoS2-catalyzed supercritical CO2 chemistry feeds the same surface acrolein and Strecker corridor.

pathways.name: Fully spark-driven precursor supply to methionine
pathways.description: Volcanic lightning provides methanethiol, HCN, formaldehyde, and acetaldehyde; aldehydes yield acrolein, which then passes through methional and methionine aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Direct discharge acrolein with hydrothermal FeS methanethiol
pathways.description: Acrolein generated directly in discharge chemistry reacts with hydrothermal FeS-derived methanethiol and proceeds through Strecker chemistry.

pathways.name: Direct discharge acrolein with MoS2-derived methanethiol
pathways.description: MoS2-generated methanethiol converges with directly discharge-produced acrolein before the methional-Strecker sequence.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde plus discharge formaldehyde to acrolein
pathways.description: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch chemistry supplies acetaldehyde, discharge chemistry supplies formaldehyde/HCN/NH3/methanethiol, and aldol condensation forms acrolein en route to methionine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde plus FeS methanethiol to surface Strecker methionine
pathways.description: A hydrothermal-only aldehyde and methanethiol supply converges with surface HCN/NH3 from discharge chemistry to enter the single proven methionine corridor.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde plus MoS2 methanethiol to surface Strecker methionine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde and high-yield MoS2-derived methanethiol meet surface-derived HCN and formaldehyde, converging on acrolein and then methional.

pathways.name: HCN reservoir pathway through formamide buffering
pathways.description: Discharge chemistry generates HCN, which is buffered through formamide chemistry before being consumed in the methional Strecker step.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal methanethiol plus HCN-reservoir surface route to methionine
pathways.description: FeS-derived methanethiol is combined with surface aldehyde chemistry and HCN buffered through formamide, then funneled through methional and methionine aminonitrile.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong literature-aligned route. The acrolein -> methional -> aminonitrile -> methionine corridor is the only well-supported methionine pathway and is correctly used. Main weakness is the hydrothermal FeS methanethiol source, which is experimentally supported but low-yield and geochemically constrained.
suggestions: Specify FeS or mackinawite explicitly rather than generic FeS-mediated wording
Constrain hydrothermal temperatures to <=120C-equivalent conditions to respect methionine thermal lability
Annotate methanethiol as the bottleneck precursor

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Best aligned with the supplied literature. MoS2/supercritical CO2 methanethiol generation is directly supported by recent results and feeds the validated methional-Strecker pathway cleanly.
suggestions: Include the documented ~200C and extended reaction duration for MoS2 methanethiol synthesis
Clarify transfer/quenching from hydrothermal generation to cooler surface Strecker conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Most directly supported by Miller/Van Trump and Parker/Cleaves/Bada-style evidence. A single discharge environment producing the needed precursor set, followed by acrolein formation and Strecker conversion, is highly coherent.
suggestions: State the reducing gas composition and discharge context more explicitly
Separate product generation in rxn_003 into more granular reactions for mechanistic clarity

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Target is reached through the validated downstream corridor, but the direct generation of acrolein in rxn_006 is less secure than the aldehyde-condensation route emphasized in the literature.
suggestions: Prefer explicit aldehyde-to-acrolein condensation rather than direct acrolein appearance
Add evidence or constraints for discharge-derived acrolein formation

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A plausible hybrid route, strengthened by the MoS2 methanethiol source, but still weakened by the less-established direct acrolein production step.
suggestions: Replace rxn_006 with aldehyde generation plus rxn_005 wherever possible
Document how acrolein is preserved prior to methanethiol addition

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good convergence on acrolein from mixed hydrothermal/surface precursor supply. The weakness is rxn_004, which bundles acetaldehyde and propionaldehyde production from FTT chemistry without strong methionine-specific literature support.
suggestions: Provide mineral/catalyst details and narrower product expectations for hydrothermal aldehyde formation
Justify propionaldehyde generation or remove it if unused in the pathway

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Network connectivity is good and the pathway converges appropriately, but it depends on two weaker hydrothermal precursor-generating steps before entering the validated methionine corridor.
suggestions: Use MoS2 methanethiol generation instead of FeS for a stronger literature footing
Tighten hydrothermal conditions to avoid invoking overly hot vent settings

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A solid convergent pathway. MoS2-derived methanethiol substantially improves plausibility, while the hydrothermal aldehyde contribution remains the principal uncertainty.
suggestions: Support rxn_004 with explicit magnetite or Fe3O4 FTT conditions
Clarify whether acetaldehyde is the intended useful product and treat propionaldehyde as byproduct

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The target is still obtained, but the HCN reservoir/formamide buffering concept is weakly represented. rxn_010 is chemically underspecified and effectively self-loops HCN while producing formamide, so its contribution to pathway logic is limited.
suggestions: Redefine rxn_010 as a distinct HCN <-> formamide interconversion with clear net effect
Add concentration, UV, or drying context to justify reservoir behavior

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route combines the weakest hydrothermal methanethiol source with the least well-defined HCN-buffering step. The core methionine-forming corridor remains strong, but the upstream support chemistry is comparatively soft.
suggestions: Swap FeS methanethiol generation for MoS2-supported chemistry
Rebuild the HCN reservoir step with explicit literature support or remove it

evaluation.network_feedback:
This is a good but not approval-grade synthesis network. Topologically it is well connected, includes sufficient molecule and reaction nodes, and correctly identifies strong hubs around HCN, methanethiol, acrolein, methional, and methionine aminonitrile. The central achievement is strong literature alignment on the only experimentally verified methionine route: acrolein formation, Michael addition of methanethiol to methional, Strecker conversion to the aminonitrile, and hydrolysis to methionine. That core corridor is chemically sound and well justified by the supplied literature. The network also captures the key bottleneck identified in the literature, namely methanethiol availability, and appropriately includes hydrothermal FeS-based and MoS2-based upstream sources as convergent support routes. Environmental interconnection is generally good, especially in routes where hydrothermal methanethiol generation feeds surface acrolein/Strecker chemistry and then biochemical hydrolysis.

However, the network falls short of excellent because several upstream reactions are oversimplified or insufficiently supported. rxn_006 treats acrolein as a direct discharge/photochemical product, whereas the literature emphasizes aldol condensation from formaldehyde and acetaldehyde as the validated route. rxn_004 bundles hydrothermal FTT production of acetaldehyde and propionaldehyde with limited methionine-specific support. rxn_010 is the weakest element: its HCN -> formamide, HCN formulation is mechanistically unclear and contributes little net chemistry. There is also some inconsistency with the strict catalyst specification requirement, since hydrothermal reactions invoke FeS and MoS2 rather than only the prescribed list, though chemically these choices are literature-appropriate for methionine. The biochemical side branch to homocysteine/thiolactone is literature-motivated but underspecified and not integrated into target achievement. Finally, the provided summary lacks explicit per-reaction citations and detailed conditions, which would lower literature-alignment confidence under the original generation rubric.

Overall, the network is viable and chemically coherent around the target, but the total package is not strong enough for >=0.9 approval because of upstream speculative steps, underspecified conditions, and incomplete adherence to strict schema/citation requirements.
